text.title: Solo Utilization and Pipeline Dashboard Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for a freelance utilization-and-pipeline dashboard that drives quarterly rate decisions.

rule.statement: The dashboard contains exactly four headline metrics: (1) weekly utilization % = billable hours / capacity hours; (2) realised $/hr = billed revenue in period / total worked hours in period; (3) pipeline coverage = (signed + qualified next-90d revenue) / 90-day revenue target; (4) rate trend = rolling-3-month realised $/hr. No fifth headline metric.
rule.rationale: Above four headline metrics the freelancer skips review or fixates on a vanity number. The four chosen each drive a distinct decision (capacity, pricing, sales motion, rate trajectory).

rule.statement: Realised $/hr divides billed revenue by TOTAL worked hours, including admin, sales, marketing, invoicing, and project-management overhead. Dividing by billable-only hours is forbidden — it produces flattering numbers that mislead rate decisions.
rule.rationale: Most freelancers' hourly rate looks fine on billable-only math and breaks on total-time math. Rate decisions made on flattering math are the proximate cause of feast-or-famine cycles.

rule.statement: Pipeline coverage uses a fixed 90-day forward window. "Signed" requires a signed SOW or deposit paid; "qualified" requires a named decision-maker, a confirmed budget range, and a stated decision date. Speculative leads ("might do something with us next year") are excluded.
rule.rationale: Unbounded pipeline definitions inflate coverage and trigger the "I'm fine on pipeline" comfort feeling right before a 6-week drought.

rule.statement: The dashboard is reviewed once per calendar quarter, on a fixed date (e.g., second Monday of Q-start month). The review produces exactly one of three outcomes: (a) raise floor rate, (b) hold floor rate, (c) drop a non-fit client. Outcomes that ARE forbidden: "monitor for another quarter", "depends on the economy", "let's see how next month goes".
rule.rationale: Vague outcomes are the failure mode that lets a stale rate persist for 3 years. Forcing a discrete decision converts data into action.

rule.statement: Any rate change MUST cite the specific metric that triggered it (e.g., "realised $/hr rose to $138 in Q1 vs floor of $125 → raise floor to $140"). Rate changes justified by "feeling underpaid" or "competitor charges more" without a data citation are rejected.
rule.rationale: Data-cited rate decisions survive client pushback ("the market is soft"). Feeling-driven decisions collapse in negotiation.

example.code:
Q1 2026 — Dashboard review (2026-04-08)
Utilization (rolling 12w): 71% (target 65–75) → ok Realised $/hr: $138 (floor $125) → above floor Pipeline coverage (90d): 1.4x (target ≥1.2x) → ok Rate trend (3m): $122 → $128 → $138 → up
DECISION: Raise floor rate from $125 → $140 effective Q2 2026. Trigger: r5 cite — realised $138 sustained over 3 months, pipeline coverage >1.2x means no scarcity pressure, utilization upper-band means we're capacity constrained — classic raise signal.
example.note: Four metrics, total-time denominator, 90d pipeline window, discrete decision, data-cited rationale.

antipattern.description: Freelancer reviews "billable hours $/hr" (excluding 8 admin hours/week), sees $185, holds rate flat, then has 3 quiet weeks and panics into discount work at $90.
antipattern.reason: Violates r2 (admin excluded from denominator) and r4 (no discrete decision when pipeline coverage was actually thin). Flattering math hid the actual exposure.
